definition: A push or pull acting on an object that can cause it to accelerate, decelerate, or change direction. Forces are vectors — they have both size and direction.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Pushing a shopping trolley applies a force. By Newton's second law: \(F = ma\), so a \(10\) kg trolley pushed with \(20\) N accelerates at \(2\) m/s².

definition: The amount of matter in an object. Mass doesn't change depending on where you are — it's the same on Earth as on the Moon.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A bag of flour has a mass of \(1\) kg regardless of whether it's on Earth or in space.

definition: The capacity of a system to do work. Energy comes in many forms — kinetic, potential, thermal, chemical — and can be converted between them but never created or destroyed.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A compressed spring stores elastic potential energy. When released, it converts to kinetic energy.

definition: The rate at which an object's velocity changes over time. It can mean speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A car going from \(0\) to \(20\) m/s in \(4\) s has acceleration \(a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{20}{4} = 5\) m/s².

definition: The speed of an object in a specified direction. Unlike speed (a scalar), velocity is a vector — direction matters.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A car travelling north at \(30\) m/s has a velocity of \(+30\) m/s north. If it reverses, its velocity is \(-30\) m/s even though its speed is unchanged.

definition: How fast an object is moving, without regard to direction. Speed is the magnitude of velocity.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A sprinter running \(100\) m in \(10\) s has an average speed of \(\frac{100}{10} = 10\) m/s.

definition: The straight-line distance from start to finish in a specific direction. Unlike distance, it accounts for direction and can be negative.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: If you walk \(5\) m east then \(5\) m west, your displacement is \(0\) m, but your distance travelled is \(10\) m.

definition: An object stays at rest or continues moving in a straight line at constant velocity unless acted on by an unbalanced external force. This is the law of inertia.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A ball rolling on a frictionless surface keeps rolling at the same speed forever, because no net force acts on it.

definition: The net force on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration. A larger force causes greater acceleration; a heavier object is harder to accelerate.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(5\) kg object acted on by a net force of \(20\) N accelerates at \(a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{20}{5} = 4\) m/s².

definition: For every action (force) there is an equal and opposite reaction. Forces always come in pairs acting on two different objects.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: When you push off a wall, the wall pushes back on you with the same force in the opposite direction — that's what moves you away.

definition: The gravitational force exerted on an object by the Earth (or another body). Weight depends on location; mass does not.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(60\) kg person on Earth has weight \(W = mg = 60 \times 9.81 \approx 589\) N. On the Moon (\(g \approx 1.6\) m/s²), their weight would be only \(96\) N.

definition: The attractive force between any two objects with mass. Near Earth's surface, it gives objects a downward acceleration of approximately \(9.81\) m/s².
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A ball dropped from rest accelerates downward at \(g = 9.81\) m/s². After \(2\) s it has speed \(v = gt = 19.62\) m/s.

definition: The tendency of an object to resist changes to its state of motion. A more massive object has more inertia and is harder to start moving or stop.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A bowling ball and a tennis ball dropped together fall at the same rate, but the bowling ball is much harder to push across a floor — it has more inertia.

definition: A measure of how much motion an object has. It equals mass multiplied by velocity and is conserved in collisions when no external forces act.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(2\) kg ball moving at \(5\) m/s has momentum \(p = mv = 2 \times 5 = 10\) kg·m/s.

definition: The change in momentum of an object, equal to force multiplied by the time for which it acts.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A force of \(10\) N applied for \(3\) s gives an impulse of \(J = F \Delta t = 30\) N·s, changing momentum by \(30\) kg·m/s.

definition: The energy an object has because it is moving. It depends on both mass and velocity, and grows with the square of speed.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(4\) kg ball moving at \(3\) m/s has \(KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}(4)(9) = 18\) J.

definition: Stored energy that an object has because of its position or state. The most common type at first-year level is gravitational potential energy.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(2\) kg book placed on a shelf \(1.5\) m high has gravitational PE: \(U = mgh = 2 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \approx 29.4\) J.

definition: The energy transferred to or from an object when a force moves it through a distance. Work is only done when the force has a component in the direction of motion.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Pushing a box with a force of \(15\) N over \(4\) m: \(W = Fd = 15 \times 4 = 60\) J. If the force is at angle \(\theta\), then \(W = Fd\cos\theta\).

definition: The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. A more powerful engine doesn't necessarily do more work — it does the same work faster.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A motor does \(600\) J of work in \(5\) s. Its power is \(P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{600}{5} = 120\) W.

definition: The total energy in a closed system remains constant. Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A ball dropped from height \(h\) converts all potential energy to kinetic energy just before hitting the ground: \(mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\), so \(v = \sqrt{2gh}\).

definition: A force that opposes relative motion between two surfaces in contact. It acts parallel to the surface and opposite to the direction of motion.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Sliding a wooden block across a table: \(f = \mu_k N\), where \(\mu_k\) is the kinetic friction coefficient and \(N\) is the normal force.

definition: The contact force that a surface exerts on an object, acting perpendicular to the surface. It stops objects from passing through solid surfaces.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(10\) kg book resting on a horizontal table: the normal force is \(N = mg = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1\) N upward.

definition: The force exerted per unit area on a surface. The same force spread over a smaller area creates greater pressure.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A force of \(100\) N on an area of \(0.5\) m² gives pressure \(P = \frac{F}{A} = 200\) Pa.

definition: The mass of a substance per unit volume. Denser materials pack more mass into the same space.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Water has density \(\rho = 1000\) kg/m³. A \(2\) m³ block of water has mass \(m = \rho V = 1000 \times 2 = 2000\) kg.

definition: The distance between two successive identical points on a wave, for example from one crest to the next.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Visible light has wavelengths between approximately \(400\) nm (violet) and \(700\) nm (red).

definition: The number of complete wave cycles that pass a point per second.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A tuning fork vibrating at \(440\) Hz completes \(440\) cycles per second, producing the musical note A.

definition: The time taken for one complete cycle of a wave or oscillation. Period and frequency are reciprocals of each other.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A wave with frequency \(5\) Hz has period \(T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2\) s.

definition: The speed at which a wave travels through a medium. It equals frequency multiplied by wavelength.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A wave with frequency \(200\) Hz and wavelength \(1.5\) m has speed \(v = f\lambda = 200 \times 1.5 = 300\) m/s.

definition: A fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force in an electric field. Charge can be positive (protons) or negative (electrons).
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: An electron carries charge \(q = -1.6 \times 10^{-19}\) C. A proton carries \(+1.6 \times 10^{-19}\) C.

definition: The rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor. Conventional current flows from positive to negative.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: If \(10\) C of charge flows through a wire in \(2\) s, the current is \(I = \frac{Q}{t} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\) A.

definition: The electric potential difference between two points — the energy per unit charge needed to move a charge between those points. Often called electromotive force (EMF) for a battery.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A \(9\) V battery gives \(9\) J of energy to every coulomb of charge that passes through it.

definition: A measure of how much a component opposes the flow of electric current. Higher resistance means less current for a given voltage.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A component with resistance \(10\) Ω connected to \(5\) V allows current \(I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5\) A.

definition: The voltage across a component equals the current through it multiplied by its resistance, provided temperature is constant.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A resistor of \(50\) Ω carries a current of \(0.2\) A. The voltage across it is \(V = IR = 0.2 \times 50 = 10\) V.

definition: The branch of physics dealing with heat, temperature, and their relation to energy and work. The four laws of thermodynamics govern how energy moves and transforms.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A heat engine converts thermal energy into mechanical work — car engines and power stations are real-world applications.

definition: A measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. Higher temperature means particles move faster.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Water boils at \(100°C = 373\) K. Converting: \(T(\text{K}) = T(°C) + 273\).

definition: Thermal energy transferred between objects due to a temperature difference. Heat flows from hotter objects to cooler ones.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: When a hot metal rod is placed in cold water, heat flows from the rod to the water until both reach the same temperature.

definition: The amount of energy needed to raise the temperature of \(1\) kg of a substance by \(1\) K (or \(1°C\)). Different materials require different amounts of heat.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Water has \(c = 4200\) J/(kg·K). Heating \(2\) kg of water by \(10°C\) requires \(Q = mc\Delta T = 2 \times 4200 \times 10 = 84{,}000\) J.

definition: The bending of a wave (usually light) as it passes from one medium to another with a different density or optical property. The speed of the wave changes, causing the direction to change.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: A straw placed in a glass of water appears bent because light travels slower in water than in air, changing direction at the surface.

definition: The spontaneous emission of particles or energy from an unstable atomic nucleus as it transforms into a more stable form.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: Carbon-14 undergoes beta decay, emitting an electron and converting a neutron to a proton. This is used in radiocarbon dating.

definition: The time taken for half of the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay. After each half-life, the activity halves.
subject: introduction-to-physics
examples: If a sample has \(800\) atoms and a half-life of \(10\) years, after \(10\) years \(400\) remain; after \(20\) years \(200\) remain.
